title: Fueling Your Head
description: Nutritional anxiety is a distinct psychological construct — not just 'eat right.'

blocks.content: Mile 70 of a 100-mile race. You reach for your next gel. It's not there. You used your last one 20 minutes ago and forgot to restock at the last aid station. What happens next isn't in your stomach — it's in your head. 'I'm going to bonk.' That thought lands like a brick, and the cascade begins: heart rate ticks up from anxiety, breathing goes shallow, focus narrows from race execution to nutrition crisis.

Meanwhile, you probably have enough glycogen for another 30 minutes. The next aid station is in 15. The crisis is not physiological. It's psychological. But by the time your brain finishes spiraling, it WILL be physiological — because anxiety just spiked your cortisol, which is about to wreck your gut.
By the end of this lesson, you will have rehearsed your exact race-day nutrition plan in training.

blocks.question: The Grit Stack steps: Breathe → ___ → Reframe → ___ → Segment.
blocks.explanation: Correct. Breathe → Detach → Reframe → Execute → Segment. The full sequence in about 30 seconds.

blocks.content: Nutritional anxiety is a distinct psychological construct — not just 'eat right.' Your brain's BELIEF about your fuel state affects performance independently of your actual fuel state. You can eat perfectly and still bonk because your brain decided you weren't.

items.title: The Bonking Cascade
items.content: Low glucose impairs the prefrontal cortex — the coach. With the coach offline, the chimp runs unchecked, amplifying negative spirals. The bonking cascade isn't just about calories. It's a feedback loop between brain and gut, and the entry point is often psychological, not physiological.

items.title: Stress → Cortisol → Gut → GI Distress → More Stress
items.content: Cortisol from anxiety redirects blood away from the digestive system. Gastric emptying slows. Food sits in your stomach. You feel bloated, nauseous, crampy. You stop eating — making the eventual bonk worse. The fear of the bonk caused the bonk.

items.title: 20-50% GI Distress Prevalence
items.content: Between 20 and 50 percent of endurance athletes experience gut distress during competition (de Oliveira, Burini, & Jeukendrup, 2014). The kicker: psychological stress was identified as a major contributor. Not bad food choices. Stress itself.

items.title: Nutritional Anxiety (Nutrients, 2025)
items.content: Viribay et al. identified three failure patterns: anticipatory fear of dietary mistakes, internal negotiation between knowledge and situational trust ('What if today's different?'), and ritualization as psychological regulation — rigid routines that shatter when disrupted, because the plan was a coping mechanism, not just a nutrition strategy.

blocks.question: 'Nothing new on race day' is a psychological rule because:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Your BRAIN needs to be familiar with the fuel. Without repeated evidence that the plan works, the brain defaults to uncertainty — and uncertainty is where the chimp lives.

options.text: New foods always cause stomach problems
Unfamiliar nutrition adds decision load and anxiety — your brain needs evidence from repeated successful practice to trust the plan
Your body can only absorb foods it has processed before

items.label: Build Your Exact Race Nutrition Plan
items.detail: Grams of carbs per hour, fluid intake, timing, brands, what you carry vs. what you grab at aid stations. We're not here to design it. We're here to bulletproof it psychologically.

items.label: Execute the Exact Plan in 3 Training Sessions
items.detail: Same products. Same timing. Same quantities. Same intensity. Each successful rehearsal builds evidence: 'This plan works. I've done it before. My body handles it.' Three data points that say otherwise when the chimp whispers 'what if your stomach can't handle it.'

items.label: Deliberately Practice a Disruption
items.detail: In one rehearsal, intentionally break the plan. Skip a gel. Delay a bottle. Then build your If-Then: 'If I miss a feeding window, I will take my next intake 15 minutes early.' 'If my stomach rejects a gel, I switch to liquid calories in small sips.' Pre-programmed responses for the most common fueling disruptions.

items.label: On Race Day, Execute — Deploy the Grit Stack for Nutritional Crises
items.detail: When the cascade starts: Breathe (one 6-2-7). Detach ('That's the chimp catastrophizing about one missed gel'). Reframe ('I have enough fuel for the next 30 minutes. This is a hiccup, not a crisis'). Execute ('Eat what I have. Drink what I have. Keep moving'). Segment ('Next aid station. That's my only job').

blocks.content: Commitment: I will rehearse my race nutrition plan in my next 3 long sessions.
'Nothing new on race day' is psychological, not just physiological.
Next — what happens after the finish line.
